general_note: Cats are masters at hiding illness as they are both predator and prey in the wild. By the time symptoms appear they may be more unwell than they look. Any sudden behaviour change warrants attention.

symptoms.description: A cat refusing food for more than 24 hours is a medical concern. Cats cannot safely fast like dogs - even 2-3 days without eating can trigger hepatic lipidosis (fatty liver disease).

possible_causes.brief: Loss of smell = loss of appetite
Usually causes increased appetite but can swing

home_care: Try warming food slightly to release aromas
Offer strongly-smelling favourites (tuna, sardines, chicken)
Hand-feed small portions
Ensure quiet, safe eating environment away from other pets
Check mouth for ulcers, broken teeth, or red gums
Keep water fresh and try different bowl/fountain

vet_urgency.low: One missed meal but eating treats and acting normal - monitor
vet_urgency.medium: Reduced appetite for 24 hours - phone vet for advice
vet_urgency.high: Not eating for 24+ hours, especially overweight cat - vet within 24h (hepatic lipidosis risk)

red_flags: Complete refusal of food for 24+ hours
Combined with vomiting or weight loss
Overweight cat not eating (high hepatic lipidosis risk)

combinations.concern: Likely kidney disease or diabetes - vet bloods

symptoms.description: Forceful ejection of stomach contents. Occasional vomiting (e.g. hairballs) is common in cats but frequent or persistent vomiting is not normal.

possible_causes.brief: Often with lethargy and appetite loss

home_care: Withhold food for 6-12 hours (NOT longer - cats need food)
Offer small amounts of water frequently
Reintroduce bland diet: plain boiled chicken, white fish
Small frequent meals rather than one large
Use hairball remedy paste if appropriate
Groom regularly to reduce hair ingestion

vet_urgency.low: Occasional hairball or single vomit, cat otherwise well - monitor
vet_urgency.medium: More than 2-3 vomits in a day, or persistent over multiple days - vet appointment
vet_urgency.high: Continuous vomiting, blood, suspected string ingestion, lethargy, or 24+ hours without keeping food/water down - urgent vet

red_flags: Blood in vomit (red or coffee-grounds appearance)
String/thread visible from mouth or anus (NEVER PULL - go to vet)

combinations.concern: Likely kidney disease or diabetes - vet
Possible pancreatitis or serious illness - urgent vet

symptoms.description: Frequent, loose, or watery bowel movements. Can quickly cause dehydration in cats.

possible_causes.brief: Many human meds toxic to cats

home_care: Continue offering small frequent meals of bland food (chicken)
Do NOT fast cats with diarrhoea for long
Ensure constant fresh water access
Use probiotic paste (vet-approved)
Check faeces sample for vet if persistent
Worm with appropriate product if overdue

vet_urgency.low: Single soft stool, cat eating normally - monitor 24h
vet_urgency.medium: Persistent for 24-48 hours - phone vet for advice
vet_urgency.high: Bloody diarrhoea, severe dehydration, kitten with diarrhoea - urgent vet

symptoms.description: Reduced activity, less interaction, sleeping more than normal. In cats this is often a key early sign of illness.
home_care: Provide quiet, warm, comfortable resting area
Ensure water and food are easily accessible
Note specific changes: hiding more? not jumping?
Check gum colour (should be pink and moist)
Monitor breathing rate (normal 20-30 at rest)
Keep stress and other pets at bay

vet_urgency.low: Slightly more sleepy after busy day, eating well - monitor
vet_urgency.medium: Persistent lethargy over 24 hours with reduced appetite - vet appointment
vet_urgency.high: Severe lethargy, collapse, pale gums, breathing difficulty - urgent vet

red_flags: Pale, white, blue, or yellow gums
Combined with not eating or vomiting

combinations.concern: Kidney disease, diabetes, or hyperthyroidism - vet bloods

symptoms.description: Cat retreating to unusual or hidden spaces, avoiding interaction. Often the first sign of illness in cats.

possible_causes.brief: May hide for days settling in

home_care: Provide safe, accessible hiding places
Don't force interaction
Check resources (food, water, litter) are accessible to the hiding cat
Quiet observation for any other symptoms
Use Feliway plug-in diffuser if stress-related
Note timing - sudden hiding more concerning than gradual

vet_urgency.low: Recent change in household and cat hiding briefly - normal
vet_urgency.medium: Persistent hiding for more than 24 hours - phone vet for advice
vet_urgency.high: Hiding with not eating, not moving, other illness signs - urgent vet

red_flags: Hiding in cold or hard places (sign of pain)

symptoms.description: Notable increase in water consumption. Cats normally drink small amounts; visible drinking increase is significant.
home_care: Measure water intake daily
Track urination frequency and clumping size
Weigh cat weekly
Encourage drinking with water fountains
Consider mixing wet food into diet
Note other symptoms (appetite, energy)

vet_urgency.low: Slight increase with dry diet or warm weather - monitor
vet_urgency.medium: Sustained increase over days/weeks with no clear cause - vet for bloods/urine
vet_urgency.high: Drinking and urinating excessively with weight loss, vomiting, lethargy - urgent vet

combinations.concern: Likely hyperthyroidism or diabetes - vet bloods

symptoms.description: Larger or more frequent urination. Usually accompanies increased thirst.

possible_causes.brief: Most common cause in older cats

home_care: Provide multiple litter trays (one per cat + one extra)
Keep litter trays clean and accessible
Track clump size and frequency
Encourage water intake
Collect urine sample if possible (non-absorbent litter)
Watch for any straining or signs of pain

vet_urgency.low: Mild increase with no other symptoms - monitor
vet_urgency.medium: Persistent increase with weight changes or thirst - vet for tests
vet_urgency.high: Straining with no output (URGENT especially in males), blood in urine - emergency vet

red_flags: Straining in litter tray with no/little urine (URGENT)

combinations.concern: Kidney disease, diabetes, hyperthyroidism - vet bloods
FLUTD, infection, or stones - vet within 24h

symptoms.description: Urinating or defaecating outside the litter tray, especially in a previously trained cat. NEVER just behavioural - always rule out medical causes first.

possible_causes.brief: Type of litter, location, cleanliness, size

home_care: Take to vet first to rule out medical causes (always)
Provide one litter tray per cat plus one extra
Place trays in quiet, accessible locations
Clean trays at least daily
Use unscented, fine clumping litter
Try low-sided tray for older or arthritic cats
Reduce stressors (Feliway diffusers)
Do not punish - causes worse stress

vet_urgency.low: (no low - always investigate)
vet_urgency.medium: New issue with no obvious distress - vet appointment within days
vet_urgency.high: Straining with no urine (especially males), blood, crying in tray - EMERGENCY

red_flags: Male cat straining with no urine output (BLOCKAGE - 24h to fatal)

combinations.concern: Possible diabetes or kidney disease - vet

symptoms.description: Repeated attempts to urinate with little or no output. In male cats, urinary blockage can be fatal within 24-48 hours.

possible_causes.brief: EMERGENCY - kidney failure within 24h if untreated

home_care: STRAINING WITH NO OUTPUT = EMERGENCY (especially males) - go to vet NOW
Do not try home remedies
Note when last successful urination was
Check abdomen gently - hard bladder is critical sign
Keep cat as calm as possible during transport

vet_urgency.low: (no low category for true straining)
vet_urgency.medium: Female cat with mild straining, some output - vet within 24h
vet_urgency.high: Male cat straining with no output, distressed cat crying - EMERGENCY vet IMMEDIATELY (life-threatening)

red_flags: Any male cat unable to produce urine
Hard, large bladder palpable in abdomen
Repeated visits to tray with no result
Lethargy and vomiting with urinary signs
Cold, weak - signs of impending collapse

symptoms.description: Frequent or persistent sneezing, often a sign of upper respiratory infection (cat flu).

possible_causes.brief: Grass seed - sudden onset, one nostril

home_care: Keep cat warm and stress-free
Steam therapy: sit cat in steamy bathroom (NOT a hot bath)
Wipe discharge gently with damp cloth
Warm food to enhance smell and encourage eating
Ensure hydration
Isolate from other cats if multi-cat household

vet_urgency.low: Occasional sneeze, otherwise well - monitor
vet_urgency.medium: Persistent sneezing for more than 3 days with discharge - vet appointment
vet_urgency.high: Cat not eating, severe lethargy, breathing difficulty, kitten with severe symptoms - urgent vet

red_flags: Not eating (loss of smell prevents appetite)

symptoms.description: Discharge from one or both eyes, ranging from clear watering to thick discharge with squinting or redness.
home_care: Wipe gently with cooled boiled water and gauze
Use separate gauze for each eye
Do not use any human eye drops
Prevent rubbing (Elizabethan collar if needed)
Keep cat indoors if outdoor cat
Quarantine from other cats (contagious)

vet_urgency.low: Slight tearing, eye comfortable - clean and monitor
vet_urgency.medium: Persistent discharge with redness - vet appointment within 24-48h
vet_urgency.high: Eye held closed, cloudy eye, severe pain, blood in eye - urgent vet

red_flags: Combined with cat flu signs and not eating

symptoms.description: Coughing is much less common in cats than dogs. Often confused with hairball retching, but real coughing is concerning.

possible_causes.brief: Wheezing cough, may mimic hairball retching
Often presents as breathing difficulty rather than cough

home_care: Note pattern: episodic? After exercise? Continuous?
Reduce environmental triggers (smoke, perfumes, dust, scented litters)
Use HEPA air purifier if asthma suspected
Video the cough for the vet to see
Reduce stress
Keep up parasite prevention

vet_urgency.low: Occasional cough, otherwise well - monitor
vet_urgency.medium: Recurring cough over days - vet appointment
vet_urgency.high: Cough with breathing difficulty, open-mouth breathing, blue gums - EMERGENCY

combinations.concern: Asthma attack or heart failure - EMERGENCY
Possible heartworm, lungworm, or cancer - vet
Pneumonia or systemic illness - vet within 24h

symptoms.description: Unintentional reduction in body weight. Significant in cats given their small frame.
home_care: Weigh weekly on kitchen scales
Track food intake carefully
Note water consumption
Photograph from above and side
Check mouth for dental issues
Bring fresh faeces sample to vet

vet_urgency.low: Slight loss with diet change - monitor
vet_urgency.medium: Unexplained loss over weeks - vet for full workup
vet_urgency.high: Rapid loss with other symptoms - vet within 24h

red_flags: Visible spine, hip bones, or ribs
Loss with increased thirst (kidney, diabetes, thyroid)
Loss with increased appetite (thyroid, diabetes, GI issues)

symptoms.description: Grooming so frequently or intensely that hair is being removed, leaving bald or thinning patches.

possible_causes.brief: Even one bite triggers cats with flea allergy
E.g. bladder pain causes belly licking

home_care: Check thoroughly for fleas with flea comb
Apply spot-on flea treatment (vet-approved)
Note location: symmetric? Belly only? Back?
Identify and reduce stressors
Provide enrichment (puzzle feeders, climbing, play)
Try Feliway diffuser for stress

vet_urgency.low: Mild grooming, no bald patches - normal
vet_urgency.medium: Bald patches developing or skin damage - vet appointment
vet_urgency.high: Open wounds, severe self-trauma, infected skin - urgent vet

symptoms.description: A previously well-groomed cat becoming messy, with mats, dandruff, greasy patches, or clumps of loose fur. A sign cats are unwell as they normally groom meticulously.
home_care: Gentle daily brushing to remove mats
Trust a professional groomer for severe matting
Help reach difficult areas
Wipe with damp cloth gently
Note when grooming declined and any other changes
Check mouth and joints for pain

vet_urgency.low: Slight messiness after illness recovery - monitor
vet_urgency.medium: New persistent decline in grooming - vet appointment
vet_urgency.high: Combined with severe lethargy, not eating - vet within 24h

red_flags: Combined with not eating or vomiting

combinations.concern: Dental disease - vet for dental check

symptoms.description: Favouring one or more limbs or reluctance to put weight on a leg.
home_care: Restrict to indoor area, no jumping
Examine paws gently for cuts, wounds, swelling
Look for puncture wounds (cat bites)
Cold compress for fresh injuries
NEVER give paracetamol or ibuprofen (deadly to cats)
Allow rest in soft bed

vet_urgency.low: Mild limp after activity, improving - monitor 24h
vet_urgency.medium: Persistent limp, swelling, or fight wound - vet appointment
vet_urgency.high: Cannot bear weight, hind legs paralysed, severe pain - urgent vet

red_flags: Sudden complete paralysis of hind legs (saddle thrombus EMERGENCY)
After road traffic incident (internal injuries possible)

symptoms.description: Excessive saliva production. Cats normally do not drool noticeably; persistent drooling is usually abnormal.

possible_causes.condition: Oral ulcers (from kidney disease or stomatitis)

possible_causes.brief: Some cats drool when content - usually normal

home_care: Check mouth carefully (with care, cats can bite when in pain)
Look under tongue for string or foreign body
Note any smell from mouth
Don't pull at any visible string - go to vet
Remove possible toxins from area
Encourage gentle hydration

vet_urgency.low: Happy drooling when petted - usually fine
vet_urgency.medium: Persistent drooling for hours - vet appointment
vet_urgency.high: Drooling with distress, pawing at mouth, suspected toxin - urgent vet

red_flags: Suspected lily ingestion (deadly to cats - kidneys)
String under tongue or hanging from mouth (NEVER PULL)

combinations.concern: Foreign body or oral pain - urgent vet
Toxicity - emergency vet (especially lilies for cats)
Dental disease - vet for dental check

symptoms.description: Foul-smelling breath beyond normal. Often indicates dental problems, but can signal systemic disease.

possible_causes.brief: Most common; over 70% of cats by age 3

home_care: Examine teeth and gums (carefully)
Note any red/swollen gums, broken teeth, brown tartar
Try dental treats or chews designed for cats (VOHC approved)
Toothbrushing if cat tolerates (cat toothpaste only)
Water additives can help
Book dental check at vet

vet_urgency.low: Mild bad breath, no other symptoms - mention at next check-up
vet_urgency.medium: Persistent bad smell with visible tartar/redness - vet for dental assessment
vet_urgency.high: Foul breath with reduced eating, drooling, or systemic illness - urgent vet

red_flags: Visible growths or ulcers in mouth

combinations.concern: Kidney disease or diabetes - vet bloods

symptoms.description: Any new growth or swelling under or on the skin.

possible_causes.brief: Hot, painful, may burst - typical of outdoor cats
Lumps at vaccine sites need investigation

home_care: Photograph and measure lump
Note location, size, mobility, pain
Check for other lumps
Do NOT squeeze or pop (especially abscesses)
Keep cat from licking (E-collar if needed)
Note any vaccine history at that site

vet_urgency.low: Small lipoma in older cat - mention at next check
vet_urgency.medium: Any new lump - vet for examination and likely needle sample
vet_urgency.high: Hot painful swelling, rapid growth, breast lump, ulcerated/bleeding lump - vet within 24-48h

red_flags: Combined with weight loss or lethargy

symptoms.description: Visible effort breathing in a cat is ALWAYS an emergency. Cats are obligate nasal breathers and rarely pant - open-mouth breathing is critical.
home_care: DIFFICULTY BREATHING IN A CAT = EMERGENCY
Place in carrier without forcing into position
Keep stress to absolute minimum
Cool, well-ventilated environment
Do not give any food or water
Do not give human medications
Transport to emergency vet IMMEDIATELY

vet_urgency.low: (no low - all true breathing difficulty is urgent)
vet_urgency.medium: (very limited - any breathing difficulty in cat needs vet)
vet_urgency.high: Open-mouth breathing, blue gums, exaggerated chest movement, hind legs cold - IMMEDIATE EMERGENCY

red_flags: Open-mouth breathing (almost always emergency in cats)
Blue or grey gums and tongue

combinations.concern: Saddle thrombus from heart disease - EMERGENCY
Asthma attack or heart failure - EMERGENCY

symptoms.description: Cats rarely breathe with their mouths open. When they do, it almost always indicates significant respiratory distress.
home_care: Open-mouth breathing in a cat = EMERGENCY (unless clearly stress that resolves)
Do not handle excessively
Cool, calm environment
Place in carrier if needed for transport
Do not delay - immediate vet

vet_urgency.low: (no low)
vet_urgency.medium: Brief mouth breathing during high stress that resolves rapidly - monitor
vet_urgency.high: Any persistent mouth breathing - EMERGENCY vet

symptoms.description: Difficulty or inability to pass stool, or absence of bowel movements for more than 48 hours.
home_care: Increase wet food, reduce dry
Add water to food
Provide cat water fountain to encourage drinking
Add a teaspoon of plain pumpkin to meals
Encourage exercise/play
Try psyllium husk (very small amounts) if vet approved
Do not give human laxatives

vet_urgency.low: Single missed bowel movement, cat otherwise normal - monitor
vet_urgency.medium: No movement 48+ hours or persistent straining - vet within 24h
vet_urgency.high: Vomiting with constipation, distressed cat, bloated abdomen, blood - urgent vet

red_flags: Straining could be urinary blockage - check

combinations.concern: Likely dehydration from kidney disease - vet
Differentiate from blocked cat - urgent if male

symptoms.description: Frequent vomiting of hair clumps. Occasional hairballs are normal, but more than once or twice a month may signal a problem.

possible_causes.brief: Hair builds up; can be cause not effect

home_care: Brush daily, especially long-haired cats
Hairball-control diet or treats
Petroleum-based hairball remedies (vet-approved)
Address fleas and over-grooming causes
Provide good hydration (wet food, fountain)
Video any 'hairball' episode - may actually be asthma

vet_urgency.low: Occasional hairball (every few months) - normal
vet_urgency.medium: Frequent hairballs (weekly+) or distressing - vet appointment
vet_urgency.high: Repeatedly retching with no production, lethargy, not eating, distressed - urgent vet

red_flags: Vomiting other than hair (food, bile, fluid)

combinations.concern: Actually asthma - vet for proper diagnosis

symptoms.description: Sudden or progressive change in behaviour, especially new aggression, fear, or withdrawal.

possible_causes.brief: Most common cause of new aggression

home_care: Note triggers and patterns
Provide hiding places and safe spaces
Don't punish - increases stress
Use Feliway diffuser
Separate cats if multi-cat conflict
Document any other changes (eating, drinking, weight)
Note when behaviour started

vet_urgency.low: Brief reaction to specific stressor that resolves - monitor
vet_urgency.medium: Persistent new aggression or behaviour change - vet for health check
vet_urgency.high: Severe aggression, disorientation, with other illness signs - urgent vet

red_flags: Severe sudden aggression with no clear trigger
Aggression when specific body area touched (pain)

symptoms.description: Significantly increased meowing, crying, yowling, or other vocalisation, especially in a previously quiet cat.

possible_causes.brief: Restlessness and yowling, especially at night

home_care: Note time of day and triggers
Ensure resources accessible (food, water, tray) especially at night
Night lights for older cats
Increase enrichment if boredom-related
Don't reward (feeding) attention-seeking vocalisation
Routine vet check for older cats with new yowling

vet_urgency.low: Greeting, hunger, or seasonal heat - normal
vet_urgency.medium: New or increased yowling especially in older cats - vet for health check
vet_urgency.high: Yowling in distress, in litter tray, with other illness signs - urgent vet

red_flags: Older cat with new persistent yowling
